title: Add continue_conversation, max_budget_usd, and max_thinking_tokens to ClaudeAgentOptions
description: Three high-value options from the Python SDK are missing from ClaudeAgentOptions. (1) continue_conversation: bool — when true, continues the most recent conversation without needing to specify a session ID. Maps to --continue CLI flag. This is the most ergonomic way to resume work without tracking session IDs. Python example: options = ClaudeAgentOptions(continue_conversation=True). (2) max_budget_usd: Option<f64> — maximum API spend in USD for this session. Maps to --max-budget-usd. Critical for production deployments to prevent runaway costs from agentic loops. (3) max_thinking_tokens: Option<u32> — limits reasoning/thinking token budget. Maps to --max-thinking-tokens. Important for balancing quality vs cost when using extended thinking models. All three should be added to ClaudeAgentOptions struct, the builder, to_cli_args(), and ClaudeClient::connect(). The continue_conversation flag is P1 because it changes the fundamental UX of session resumption — instead of storing session IDs in application code, you can just pass continue_conversation=true.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add receive_response() and support multiple send_message() calls per ClaudeClient
description: Context: After fixing the single-use message_rx issue (rusty_claw-neo), ClaudeClient should support true multi-turn conversations. Python's SDK has two distinct operations: client.query(prompt) which sends a message, and client.receive_response() which returns an async iterator over messages until a ResultMessage is received. This separation is ergonomic: you can interleave sends and receives, implement timeouts, abort mid-stream, etc. Rust should have: (1) send_message() that does NOT consume any global state but instead creates a turn-specific response stream, (2) a receive_response() equivalent that collects messages until Message::Result is seen. The current ResponseStream is a good foundation but it is handed out once and never again. Design consideration: in the Python SDK, the client internally tracks a 'current turn' via session_id. In Rust, the control flow should work with Tokio's async model. One approach: each call to send_message() returns a ResponseStream that is tied to that specific turn (either by message ID or by consuming messages until the next ResultMessage). A second approach (simpler): make message_rx an Arc<Mutex<Option<Receiver>>> that can be re-populated after each turn completes. The key insight from the Python SDK is that conversation state lives in the Claude process, not in the SDK client object — so multi-turn is just sending another user message and reading the next response.
close_reason: Closed

title: Improve SdkMcpTool with type-safe handler input using serde deserialization
description: The current ToolHandler trait receives raw JSON: async fn call(&self, args: Value) -> Result<ToolResult, ClawError>. The Python SDK's SdkMcpTool[T] has a generic type parameter where T is the handler's input type, and args are automatically deserialized to T before calling the handler. While Rust's async trait generics make this trickier than Python, we can approximate it with a TypedToolHandler<T> helper: a wrapper that serde-deserializes args: Value to T: DeserializeOwned, then calls the concrete handler with typed T. The #[claw_tool] proc-macro (already partially implemented) already generates input parameter deserialization. Extend this pattern to the non-macro path: provide a ToolHandler::typed<T: DeserializeOwned>(handler: impl Fn(T) -> impl Future<Output = Result<ToolResult, ClawError>>) that wraps the closure in a TypedToolHandler. The error message when deserialization fails should include the type name and the raw args for debugging. This is P2 because the macro path already handles typed inputs, but developers using the non-macro API must manually deserialize their args.
close_reason: Closed

title: Example: hook_callbacks.rs — HookCallback Trait & Advanced Hook Patterns
description:
# hook_callbacks.rs — High-Level Hook Callback API

## What This Covers
- HookCallback trait (hooks/callback.rs) — higher-level hook abstraction
- HookInput struct with constructors (tool_use, tool_success, tool_failure, prompt)
- HookContext struct with builders (with_session, with_tools, with_agents, with_mcp_servers)
- HookResponse constructors (allow, deny, ask, with_context, with_updated_input)
- PermissionDecision enum (Allow, Deny, Ask)

## Why This Matters
The existing hooks_guardrails.rs uses the low-level HookHandler trait (control layer).
But the SDK also exposes a higher-level HookCallback trait in the hooks module with
richer input/output types. This is the "intended" hook API for most users:
- HookInput provides structured access to tool info (not raw JSON)
- HookContext gives session and environment metadata
- HookResponse has semantic constructors (allow/deny/ask) and supports
  input modification (with_updated_input) for request rewriting
- PermissionDecision is a proper enum, not a JSON boolean

This higher-level API is more Rust-idiomatic and type-safe.

## Implementation Plan
1. Create examples/hook_callbacks.rs
2. Demonstrate:
   a. Implement HookCallback trait for a content filter
   b. Use HookInput::tool_use() to construct test inputs
   c. Use HookContext::with_session().with_tools() for rich context
   d. Return HookResponse::deny("reason") for blocked operations
   e. Return HookResponse::allow("reason").with_context("additional info")
   f. Use with_updated_input() to rewrite tool arguments (e.g., add --safe flag)
   g. Show PermissionDecision::Ask for interactive prompts
3. Contrast with the lower-level HookHandler in comments
4. Show all HookEvent variants with realistic use cases

## API Surface Covered
- HookCallback trait
- HookInput struct and constructors
- HookContext struct and builders
- HookResponse struct and constructors
- PermissionDecision enum
- All HookEvent variants (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure,
  UserPromptSubmit, Stop, SubagentStop, SubagentStart, PreCompact,
  Notification, PermissionRequest)

## Considerations
- HookCallback vs HookHandler: The former is a higher-level abstraction;
  make the distinction clear in comments
- with_updated_input() enables request rewriting — powerful but potentially confusing
- HookResponse::ask() generates a permission prompt in the CLI UI
- HookContext fields are all optional — show graceful handling
close_reason: Example file exists, is complete (80-180+ lines of working code), demonstrates the described feature with helpful comments

title: Replace bool return from CanUseToolHandler with rich PermissionResult type
description: CanUseToolHandler.can_use_tool() currently returns Result<bool, ClawError>. The Python SDK's can_use_tool callback returns PermissionResultAllow or PermissionResultDeny — rich result types with additional capabilities. PermissionResultAllow can include: updated_input (dict) — a modified version of the tool's input to use instead of the original (allows the permission handler to sanitize or transform tool args), updated_permissions (list[PermissionUpdate]) — permission rule updates to persist. PermissionResultDeny can include: interrupt (bool) — whether to interrupt the entire session, not just deny this one tool use. This is a breaking change to the CanUseToolHandler trait. Design guidance: define a PermissionDecision enum: Allow { updated_input: Option<Value> }, Deny { interrupt: bool }. Change can_use_tool return to Result<PermissionDecision, ClawError>. The control handler that dispatches can_use_tool results must then translate PermissionDecision into the appropriate control protocol response. Backward compatibility consideration: provide a SimplePermissionHandler wrapper that accepts a bool-returning closure and wraps it in PermissionDecision::Allow/Deny for users who don't need the rich features. The DefaultPermissionHandler (already implemented) should be updated to return PermissionDecision. The input mutation feature (updated_input) is particularly powerful for security use cases: a permission handler can strip dangerous arguments from bash commands before allowing them, rather than having to choose between full allow and full deny.
close_reason: Closed

title: Example: session_resume.rs — Resume & Fork Sessions
description:
# session_resume.rs — Resume, Fork, and Named Sessions

## What This Covers
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().resume(session_id) — reconnect to a prior conversation
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().fork_session(true) — branch a conversation
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().session_name("my-session") — name sessions for retrieval

## Why This Matters
Session resume is the #1 missing feature for production agents. Without it, every agent
invocation starts from scratch. Real-world agents need to:
- Resume interrupted work (crash recovery, user returns next day)
- Fork a conversation to explore alternatives without losing the original
- Name sessions for human-readable management (dashboards, logs)

The Python SDK documents these prominently. Rust users will expect parity.

## Implementation Plan
1. Create examples/session_resume.rs
2. Demonstrate a 3-phase workflow:
   a. Phase 1: Start a named session, do some work, capture the session_id from
      Message::System(SystemMessage::Init { session_id, .. })
   b. Phase 2: Resume the session using .resume(session_id), continue the conversation
   c. Phase 3: Fork the resumed session using .fork_session(true)
3. Show how to extract session_id from the Init message
4. Show error handling when resuming a non-existent session
5. Register in examples/Cargo.toml as [[example]]

## API Surface Covered
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().resume()
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().fork_session()
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().session_name()
- Message::System(SystemMessage::Init { session_id }) — extracting session_id
- Message::Result(ResultMessage::Success { session_id }) — session_id in result

## Considerations
- session_id format is opaque (UUID-like string from CLI) — don't parse it
- fork_session requires a valid resume session_id to fork FROM
- session_name is advisory, used for display/logging, not as a lookup key
- The CLI manages session storage; we just pass the ID through

title: Add create_sdk_mcp_server() convenience function for ergonomic SDK MCP server creation
description: Creating an SDK-hosted MCP server currently requires: (1) SdkMcpServerImpl::new(), (2) SdkMcpTool::new() for each tool, (3) server.register_tool() for each tool, (4) Arc::new(server), (5) client.register_mcp_message_handler(). The Python SDK has create_sdk_mcp_server(tools=[...]) which takes a list of tools and returns a configured server object. Add a create_sdk_mcp_server(name, version, tools) convenience function to the mcp_server module. This should: accept a Vec<SdkMcpTool>, create and populate the SdkMcpServerImpl, return an Arc<SdkMcpServerImpl> ready for registration. Additionally, consider adding SdkMcpServerImpl::from_tools(name, version, tools: Vec<SdkMcpTool>) as an alternative constructor. This is a pure ergonomics improvement that makes the common case (one server with several tools) much simpler to express. Also add McpSdkServerConfig support in ClaudeAgentOptions: allow users to pass an SdkMcpServerImpl directly in the mcp_servers options map (with type='sdk') so the server is automatically registered during connect() without a separate register_mcp_message_handler() call.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add ThinkingBlock.signature field; complete UserMessage with uuid and parent_tool_use_id
description: Two sets of missing fields in message types. (1) ThinkingBlock: ContentBlock::Thinking currently has only thinking: String. The Python SDK's ThinkingBlock also has signature: str — a cryptographic signature used for extended thinking verification. Without this field, the signature is silently dropped during deserialization, which could break use cases where thinking block signatures need to be verified or forwarded. (2) UserMessage completeness: the Python SDK's UserMessage includes uuid: str (unique message ID), parent_tool_use_id: Option<str> (for tool result messages), and tool_use_result: Option<bool>. These enable tracking message lineage and correlating user messages with tool invocations. uuid is especially important for the rewind_files() operation — rewinding to a specific message requires knowing its UUID. The current implementation uses a generic 'message_id' string that may not match the UUID field name from the wire format. Check the actual NDJSON wire format from the CLI to confirm field names and add the missing deserialization. Note: signature in ThinkingBlock is not just metadata — it is part of the extended thinking security model and should be preserved faithfully.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add streaming/async input support to query(): accept Stream<Message> not just String prompt
description: The Python SDK's query() function accepts prompt as either a str OR an AsyncIterable[dict] — an async generator that yields message dictionaries. This enables advanced patterns: (1) inject system context before the user message, (2) send tool results programmatically as part of the initial input, (3) build complex conversation bootstraps without needing a full ClaudeClient. The Rust query() currently only accepts impl Into<String>. The fix: accept an additional input type impl Stream<Item=Value> (or a trait object) that, if provided, sends each item as a separate NDJSON line before the prompt. The implementation should still use SubprocessCLITransport but write the stream items before closing stdin. This is a P1 because it enables important agentic patterns like injecting tool results or multi-message conversation starters into a query() call without spinning up a full interactive session.
close_reason: Closed

title: Example: tool_permissions.rs — Allow/Deny Lists & Programmatic Permissions
description:
# tool_permissions.rs -- Tool Permission Controls

## What This Covers
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().allowed_tools(vec![...]) -- whitelist tools
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().disallowed_tools(vec![...]) -- blacklist tools
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().permission_prompt_tool_allowlist(vec![...]) -- auto-approve
- client.register_can_use_tool_handler(handler) -- programmatic permission decisions
- CanUseToolHandler trait implementation
- DefaultPermissionHandler builder

## Why This Matters
This is THE most important safety feature in the SDK. Every production deployment needs tool restrictions. The README already shows allowed_tools in its code sample, but there is no runnable example that demonstrates:
- How allow/deny lists interact (deny takes precedence? or allow-only?)
- How to implement CanUseToolHandler for dynamic, context-aware permission decisions
- How permission_prompt_tool_allowlist differs from allowed_tools
- How DefaultPermissionHandler provides a batteries-included approach

Without this example, users will either:
1. Use PermissionMode::BypassPermissions (unsafe)
2. Not understand the layered permission model
3. Miss the programmatic handler entirely

## Implementation Plan
1. Create examples/tool_permissions.rs
2. Demonstrate four permission strategies:
   a. Static allowlist: Only allow Read, Glob, Grep (read-only agent)
   b. Static denylist: Block Bash but allow everything else
   c. Auto-approve list: Allow Edit without prompting, but prompt for Bash
   d. Programmatic handler: Implement CanUseToolHandler that checks tool input (e.g., allow Bash only if command does not contain "rm" or "sudo")
3. Show DefaultPermissionHandler builder usage
4. Show how CanUseToolHandler receives tool_name AND tool_input for context-aware decisions
5. Compare approaches in header comments

## API Surface Covered
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().allowed_tools()
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().disallowed_tools()
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().permission_prompt_tool_allowlist()
- ClaudeClient::register_can_use_tool_handler()
- CanUseToolHandler trait
- DefaultPermissionHandler and its builder

## Considerations
- CanUseToolHandler is DISTINCT from HookHandler -- it is a synchronous yes/no gate, while hooks are async lifecycle events. Make this distinction clear.
- The CLI enforces its own permission model; SDK permissions are additive restrictions
- permission_prompt_tool_allowlist only affects the prompt UI, not actual execution
- Test with a prompt that triggers tool use to verify permissions work

title: Wire betas Vec to CLI args via --beta flag; add SdkBeta typed constants for known beta values
description: The betas: Vec<String> field exists in ClaudeAgentOptions and has a builder method, but it is never emitted in to_cli_args() or ClaudeClient::connect(). The CLI flag is --beta <feature_name> (one per beta, NOT comma-separated). Wiring: in to_cli_args(), add: for beta in &self.betas { args.push('--beta'.to_string()); args.push(beta.clone()); }. Same in ClaudeClient::connect(). Additionally, the Python SDK types betas as list[SdkBeta] where SdkBeta is a string literal type with known values like 'context-1m-2025-08-07', 'interleaved-thinking-2025-05-07', etc. For Rust, add a SdkBeta module with const string values: pub const CONTEXT_1M: &str = 'context-1m-2025-08-07'; pub const INTERLEAVED_THINKING: &str = 'interleaved-thinking-2025-05-07'; etc. These are not an enum (betas are added/removed with CLI versions) but const strings in a module serve as discoverable documentation. Using unknown beta strings still works — the constants just help with discoverability. This is a simple wiring fix paired with a discoverability enhancement.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add can_use_tool callback field to ClaudeAgentOptions for ergonomic permission setup
description: Currently, to use custom permission logic, users must: (1) create a ClaudeClient, (2) call connect(), (3) separately call register_can_use_tool_handler(). This is verbose and error-prone — it's easy to forget step 3, or to call it after connect() when the handler is needed during initialization. The Python SDK allows can_use_tool to be set directly in ClaudeAgentOptions: options = ClaudeAgentOptions(can_use_tool=my_handler). This makes permission setup impossible to forget and co-located with other session config. Implementation: add can_use_tool: Option<Arc<dyn CanUseToolHandler>> to ClaudeAgentOptions. In ClaudeClient::connect(), if options.can_use_tool is set, register it on the ControlProtocol before calling initialize(). This follows the same pre-connect pattern already used for pending_mcp_handler. Add a builder method .can_use_tool(handler: impl CanUseToolHandler + 'static). Also consider adding hook_handlers: Vec<(String, Arc<dyn HookHandler>)> to options for the same reason — hooks should be configurable at options time, not after connect(). Note: this requires implementing PermissionResult (rusty_claw-ng3 dependent issue) first, since the handler type should use the richer return type.
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix 6 bugs found during code review
description: Fixed: (1) query.rs raw field in MessageParse error contained serde error debug instead of raw message, (2) options.rs settings_sources always hardcoded to empty ignoring user config, (3-5) client.rs connect() missing --input-format=stream-json, --settings-sources=, and many other options, (6) subprocess.rs child process handle never stored making graceful_shutdown dead code
close_reason: All 6 bugs fixed, tests passing (186 unit + 21 integration + 88 doc-tests)

title: Implement McpStdioServerConfig: command, args, env fields with full CLI wiring
description: The most common MCP server type in production is stdio — a local process launched as a child, communicating via stdin/stdout. The Python SDK's McpStdioServerConfig has fields: command (str), args (list[str]), env (dict[str,str]|None), cwd (str|None). The current Rust McpServerConfig is an empty placeholder struct. This issue implements McpStdioServerConfig with all fields. Wire-up requirements: (1) the mcp_servers HashMap in ClaudeAgentOptions needs to emit a --mcp-config JSON file (or inline JSON via --mcp-config flag) when it contains entries; (2) the JSON format matches what the Claude CLI expects for external MCP servers; (3) both to_cli_args() and ClaudeClient::connect() must emit the args. Consideration: the Claude CLI accepts --mcp-config as a path to a JSON file OR inline JSON. For SDK use, writing a temp file is safer to avoid shell escaping issues. The JSON schema is: {mcpServers: {name: {type: 'stdio', command: '...', args: [...], env: {...}}}}. Security note: env variables should not include sensitive values in logs — add a debug-safe display impl. This is P0 because any user trying to use external MCP tools (which is extremely common in production Claude Code workflows) gets a silent no-op today.
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix: initialize() timeout due to unread message channel in connect()
close_reason: Fixed initialize() timeout bug: spawned background message routing task during connect() that reads from transport message channel, routes control messages to ControlProtocol, and forwards user messages to a new channel. ResponseStream simplified to only handle user messages.

title: Example: interrupt_and_status.rs — Interrupt Execution & MCP Status

# interrupt_and_status.rs — Runtime Control Operations

## What This Covers
- client.interrupt() — cancel an in-progress response
- client.mcp_status() — query connected MCP server status

## Why This Matters
These are runtime control operations for managing a live agent:
- interrupt(): Essential for timeout enforcement, user-initiated cancellation,
  and resource management. Without it, a runaway agent can't be stopped gracefully.
- mcp_status(): Needed for health checks, dashboards, and debugging MCP
  connectivity issues.

Both are commented-out in interactive_client.rs but never actually demonstrated.

## Implementation Plan
1. Create examples/interrupt_and_status.rs
2. Demonstrate interrupt:
   a. Send a message that will take a while (e.g., "write a very long essay")
   b. Spawn a separate task that waits 2 seconds then calls client.interrupt()
   c. Show how the stream terminates after interrupt
   d. Show that the client is still usable after interrupt (send another message)
3. Demonstrate mcp_status:
   a. Register an SDK MCP server with tools
   b. After connection, call client.mcp_status()
   c. Print the returned JSON showing server and tool information
4. Both demos use ClaudeClient (not query())

## API Surface Covered
- ClaudeClient::interrupt()
- ClaudeClient::mcp_status()
- Concurrent stream consumption + interrupt via tokio::spawn

## Considerations
- interrupt() is async and sends a control message; the CLI may not stop immediately
- After interrupt, the current stream may yield an error or just end
- mcp_status() returns serde_json::Value; the format depends on CLI version
- Need to use Arc<ClaudeClient> or similar for shared access between tasks
  (check if interrupt takes &self — if so, need to handle the borrow checker)
- May need tokio::select! or a JoinHandle pattern for the interrupt demo

title: Implement typed hook input variants: replace generic HookInput with 10 per-event types
description: The current HookInput is a single generic struct with optional fields for every possible hook event. This is stringly-typed — a PreToolUse hook and a SubagentStop hook both receive a HookInput, but the fields that are populated are different, and there is no compile-time guarantee about which fields are available for which event. The Python SDK has 10 typed HookInput variants, each with only the fields relevant to that event: PreToolUseHookInput {tool_name, tool_input}, PostToolUseHookInput {tool_name, tool_input, tool_response}, PostToolUseFailureHookInput {tool_name, tool_input, error}, UserPromptSubmitHookInput {prompt, context}, StopHookInput {stop_hook_active, transcript_path}, SubagentStopHookInput {agent_id, agent_type, transcript_path}, SubagentStartHookInput {agent_id, agent_type}, PreCompactHookInput {trigger, custom_instructions, summary}, NotificationHookInput {title, message}, PermissionRequestHookInput {tool_name, tool_input, permission_suggestions, blocked_path}. Implementation approach: define a HookEventInput enum with variants for each event type, each carrying event-specific data. The HookCallback trait should change to accept HookEventInput instead of HookInput. Provide From conversions for backward compatibility. The existing HookInput struct can be kept as an internal deserialization type but the public API should use the typed enum. This is P1 because typed inputs are fundamental to writing correct hook handlers — without them, every handler must defensively check Option fields and hope they contain the right data.
close_reason: Closed

# partial_messages.rs -- Streaming Partial Messages for Real-Time UIs

## What This Covers
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().include_partial_messages(true)

## Why This Matters
Without include_partial_messages, the SDK only delivers complete messages after each assistant turn. For any real-time UI (terminal progress, web dashboard, IDE integration), users need token-by-token or chunk-by-chunk streaming. This is how ChatGPT-like typing effects work. It is a MUST for:
- CLI tools that show progress as Claude thinks
- Web apps streaming responses to browsers
- IDE integrations showing real-time suggestions
- Monitoring dashboards tracking agent activity

The Python SDK supports this. Rust users building high-performance UIs need it even more.

   a. Enable partial messages in options
   b. Process the stream showing how partial messages arrive

   e. Handle edge cases (partial tool_use blocks, partial thinking)
3. Use print!() without newline + flush for the typewriter effect
4. Show timing between partial messages for understanding throughput

## API Surface Covered
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().include_partial_messages()

- Differentiating partial vs complete messages

title: Wire output_format option to CLI args for structured JSON schema output
description: The output_format field exists in ClaudeAgentOptions (pub output_format: Option<serde_json::Value>) and has a builder method, but to_cli_args() never emits it. The Python SDK supports OutputFormat with a json_schema type: {'type': 'json_schema', 'schema': {...}}. This enables structured output where Claude's response is guaranteed to conform to a JSON schema. The CLI flag is --output-format with a value of 'json' or a JSON schema spec. Important nuance: the output format is currently HARDCODED to stream-json in to_cli_args() because the SDK requires streaming JSON for its message parsing. The user-facing output_format option controls the CONTENT format of the assistant's response, not the transport format. These are different concerns: --output-format stream-json is the transport format (always required); the structured output format is a nested instruction to Claude about how to format its response. Implementation: research the exact CLI flag for content-level structured output (may be --output-schema or a system prompt injection). Add as a separate field output_schema: Option<serde_json::Value> to avoid confusion with the transport format field. Then wire to CLI args. Also add ResultMessage.structured_output field (tracked separately) to surface the parsed structured result.

title: EPIC: Python SDK Parity — align rusty_claw with the official Anthropic Python Agent SDK
description: The Anthropic Python Agent SDK (https://github.com/anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-python) is the reference implementation for the Claude Code subprocess protocol. rusty_claw is the Rust equivalent, but a comprehensive gap analysis (2026-02-24) found 35 gaps across 11 areas. This epic tracks the full parity initiative. The goal is not slavish 1:1 replication — Rust has different idioms, ownership semantics, and type safety guarantees. The goal is FUNCTIONAL parity: anything a Python developer can do with the Python SDK, a Rust developer should be able to do with rusty_claw, expressed idiomatically in Rust. Gaps are categorized P0 (critical, blocks real use), P1 (high, missing commonly used features), P2 (medium, completeness), P3 (low, polish). The single most critical gap is that ClaudeClient.send_message() is single-use (moves the message receiver), making the client effectively one-shot when Python's client supports multi-turn conversations. The second most critical gap is that external MCP servers (stdio, SSE, HTTP) are completely unimplemented — McpServerConfig is an empty placeholder struct. This epic contains sub-issues organized into 8 clusters: (1) Multi-turn client architecture, (2) External MCP server support, (3) Options API completion, (4) Permission system enrichment, (5) Hook system typed inputs/outputs, (6) Message type completeness, (7) MCP ergonomics, (8) API polish and alignment.
close_reason: Closed

title: Implement SandboxSettings struct with all fields and wire to CLI args
description: SandboxSettings exists in ClaudeAgentOptions as an empty placeholder struct (pub struct SandboxSettings;) with no fields and no CLI arg emission. The Python SDK's SandboxSettings has meaningful fields: enabled (bool), auto_allow_bash_if_sandboxed (bool), excluded_commands (list[str]), allow_unsandboxed_commands (list[str]), network (bool|None). These control whether Claude runs in a sandboxed environment (no filesystem writes, restricted network, etc.) which is critical for safe agentic deployments in production. Implementation: (1) Add the full field set to SandboxSettings. (2) Wire to CLI args: --sandbox (flag), --no-sandbox, and related flags. (3) Add to both to_cli_args() and ClaudeClient::connect(). Background: sandboxing is a key safety feature for agentic use cases where Claude has filesystem and shell access. The sandbox mode prevents runaway agents from making irreversible changes. Not implementing this means users cannot safely deploy rusty_claw in production environments where they want to experiment with agent capabilities but need a safety net.
close_reason: Closed

title: Complete ResultMessage fields: structured_output, is_error flag, duration_api_ms
description: ResultMessage currently has duration_ms and cost_usd among its fields, but is missing fields present in the Python SDK. (1) structured_output: Option<Value> — when output_format is set to a JSON schema, the result contains the parsed structured output in this field. Without this field, users cannot access the structured result even after implementing the output_format option. (2) is_error: bool — a simple flag indicating whether the session ended with an error. Currently Rust models this via a ResultMessage::Error variant, but having a flat is_error flag enables simpler pattern matching without destructuring. (3) duration_api_ms: Option<u64> — the time spent in API calls specifically, separate from total duration_ms (which includes local processing). Useful for cost attribution and latency profiling. Note on current Rust model: ResultMessage is a struct (not an enum) in the current codebase. Check actual serialization format from CLI to ensure these fields are present in the wire format and just missing from deserialization, vs. truly absent.

title: Add AssistantMessage.error field with typed AssistantMessageError enum for API-level errors
description: The Python SDK's AssistantMessage has an error field of type AssistantMessageError | None. AssistantMessageError is a string literal type covering: 'authentication_failed', 'billing_error', 'rate_limit', 'invalid_request', 'server_error', 'unknown'. These are errors that the Claude API returns as part of the assistant message (not transport/connection errors). Example: if the account hits a rate limit mid-session, the CLI surfaces this as an AssistantMessage with an error field rather than crashing. Currently, the Rust AssistantMessage has no error field. When such errors occur, they are either silently dropped or surface as unparseable messages. Implementation: add error: Option<AssistantMessageError> to AssistantMessage. Define AssistantMessageError as an enum with serde rename_all='snake_case'. Handle deserialization gracefully — unknown error types should map to AssistantMessageError::Unknown(String) rather than failing deserialization. This is important for production reliability: billing errors and rate limits are common in long-running agentic workflows, and applications need to catch and handle them rather than mysteriously failing.

title: Enrich HookMatcher with timeout field, hooks event array, and wildcard pattern matching
description: The current HookMatcher only has tool_name: Option<String> for exact matching. The Python SDK's HookMatcher is richer: matcher (str, supports glob patterns like mcp__*), hooks (list[HookEvent] — which events this matcher responds to), timeout (int — max ms before hook times out). Three things to add: (1) Wildcard/glob matching: the codebase already has a TODO comment for this. Implement glob-style matching for tool_name: mcp__* matches any MCP tool, bash* matches any bash variant. Use the glob crate or implement simple * prefix/suffix matching. (2) hooks: Vec<HookEvent> — a single HookMatcher can specify which events it responds to, instead of requiring separate matchers per event. (3) timeout: Option<Duration> — per-hook timeout. If a hook handler exceeds this, it is cancelled and the default allow/deny decision is used. The timeout field is important for production reliability: a hook that calls an external service (e.g., an audit log API) should not block the entire session indefinitely if that service is slow. Wire timeout into the hook dispatch logic in control/mod.rs using tokio::time::timeout.

title: Add transport injection to query()/ClaudeClient::new(); add get_server_info(); async RAII cleanup
description: Three ergonomics/testability improvements: (1) Transport injection: Python's query() and ClaudeSDKClient() both accept an optional transport parameter for dependency injection. In Rust, add query_with_transport(prompt, options, transport: Box<dyn Transport>) and ClaudeClient::with_transport(options, transport). This is critical for testing: mock transports allow unit tests without spawning real CLI processes. The existing integration tests in crates/rusty_claw/tests/ likely use the real CLI — having mock transport support would enable faster, deterministic unit tests. (2) get_server_info(): Python's ClaudeSDKClient has get_server_info() -> dict | None which returns CLI version and capability information. Implement as a control protocol request or by parsing the CLI --version output. Useful for runtime capability detection. (3) RAII cleanup: Python uses 'async with ClaudeSDKClient() as client:' for guaranteed cleanup. Rust does not have async Drop, but provide: (a) a close_on_drop option (bool) that spawns a background task to call close() when the ClaudeClient is dropped, and (b) a with_client(options, |client| async { ... }) helper function that calls connect() on entry and close() on exit. The with_client() pattern is the idiomatic Rust alternative to async context managers.

title: Implement typed hook output variants with system message injection, stop reason, and suppress output
description: Complementary to typed hook inputs (see typed inputs issue), hook handlers should be able to return event-specific output types. The Python SDK defines per-event output types with fields beyond the basic allow/deny/ask decision. Key missing output capabilities: (1) systemMessage: str — inject additional system context from within a hook handler (e.g., a PreToolUse hook can inject safety warnings). (2) stopReason: str — provide a reason when stopping execution. (3) suppressOutput: bool — suppress tool output from the transcript. (4) updatedMCPToolOutput: dict — modify MCP tool results from a PostToolUse hook (parallel to updatedInput for PreToolUse). The current HookResponse has updated_input for input mutation but nothing for output mutation. Design: extend the hook output enum with event-specific variants. For PostToolUse hooks, add updated_output: Option<Value>. For Stop hooks, add stop_reason: Option<String>. For all hooks, add system_message: Option<String> (injected as a system message to the conversation). This is P2 because the basic allow/deny hooks work today — this is about unlocking more powerful hook patterns. Depends on typed hook inputs issue.

title: Add developer-facing options: add_dirs, user, extra_args escape hatch, stderr callback, fallback_model, max_buffer_size
description: Several options from the Python SDK improve developer experience and production observability. (1) add_dirs: Vec<PathBuf> — additional directories Claude can access, maps to --add-dir (one flag per dir). Useful for multi-repo setups or when working outside the CWD. (2) user: Option<String> — user identifier for the session, maps to --user. Useful for audit logging and per-user permission policies. (3) extra_args: HashMap<String, Option<String>> — escape hatch for passing arbitrary CLI flags not yet modeled in the SDK. Emitted as --key value or --key (if value is None). This is important for forward compatibility: as the Claude CLI adds new flags, SDK users should not be blocked waiting for SDK updates. (4) stderr_callback: Option<Box<dyn Fn(String) + Send>> — called for each line of CLI stderr output. Currently stderr is buffered internally (stderr_buffer in SubprocessCLITransport) but never exposed to users. Useful for debugging and surfacing Claude CLI warnings. (5) fallback_model: Option<String> — model to try if the primary model is unavailable, maps to --fallback-model. (6) max_buffer_size: Option<usize> — controls stdout buffer size for large responses. Note on extra_args: this is an intentional escape hatch and should be documented as such — it bypasses type safety but prevents SDK version lock-in when the CLI gains new features.

Do not remove close() — Rust convention prefers close(). (2) setting_sources vs settings_sources: Python's ClaudeAgentOptions field is setting_sources (singular). Rust uses settings_sources (plural). The CLI flag is --setting-sources. Rename the Rust field to setting_sources to match Python. This is a breaking API change so should be done in a dedicated minor/major version bump. (3) user_message_id vs message_id: Python SDK's SDKControlRewindFilesRequest uses user_message_id as the field name. Rust's ControlRequest::RewindFiles uses message_id. title: Deep research: verify Rust SDK parity with Python agent SDK
description:
## Goal
Run a deep research agent to compare the Rusty Claw Rust SDK against the official Anthropic Python Agent SDK and identify any significant gaps, missing features, or behavioral differences.

## References
- Python SDK docs: https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/python
- Python SDK repo: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-python

## What to Check
- API surface: query(), ClaudeClient equivalent, streaming
- Options/configuration parity (ClaudeAgentOptions vs Python options)
- Message types and content block variants
- Hook/callback system equivalence
- MCP tool registration and invocation
- Session management (resume, fork, naming)
- Permission modes and tool allow/deny
- Error types and handling patterns
- Subagent support
- Any Python SDK features not yet in Rusty Claw

## Output
A structured gap analysis report listing:
1. Features present in Python SDK but missing or incomplete in Rust SDK
2. Behavioral differences (e.g. different defaults, naming conventions)
3. Features in Rust SDK not in Python SDK (extras)
4. Recommended priority order for closing gaps
notes: Research complete. Full gap analysis completed 2026-02-24. Found 35 gaps total: 3 P0 (multi-turn client, McpServerConfig implementation, mcp_servers CLI wiring), 10 P1 (continue_conversation, max_budget_usd, output_format wiring, can_use_tool inline option, typed hook inputs, permission result types, etc), 15 P2, 7 P3. 16 notable behavioral differences. 11 Rust extras vs Python. Key findings: (1) send_message() single-use is most critical architectural gap, (2) McpServerConfig is empty placeholder - external MCP servers completely non-functional, (3) several options fields (betas, sandbox_settings, permission_prompt_tool_allowlist, output_format) present in struct but never emitted in CLI args.

title: Example: external_mcp.rs — Connect to External MCP Servers
description:
# external_mcp.rs — External MCP Server Integration

## What This Covers
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().mcp_servers(HashMap<String, McpServerConfig>)
- McpServerConfig struct and its fields
- Connecting to stdio-based and SSE-based MCP servers

## Why This Matters
The existing custom_tool.rs example shows SDK MCP servers (in-process tools). But the
MCP ecosystem is much broader — users want to connect to EXTERNAL MCP servers:
- Third-party MCP tools (filesystem, database, API servers)
- Organization-internal MCP servers
- MCP servers running as separate processes (stdio transport)
- Remote MCP servers (SSE/HTTP transport)

This is how the Python SDK's mcp_servers config works. Without this example, users
only know how to create in-process tools.

## Implementation Plan
1. Create examples/external_mcp.rs
2. Demonstrate:
   a. Configure an external stdio MCP server (e.g., a simple echo server)
   b. Configure the mcp_servers HashMap with McpServerConfig
   c. Show the config structure (command, args, env for stdio servers)
   d. Show how external tools appear alongside SDK tools
3. Include a comment block explaining MCP server types (stdio vs SSE)
4. Optionally include a minimal external server script for testing

## API Surface Covered
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().mcp_servers()
- McpServerConfig struct
- External vs SDK MCP server distinction

## Considerations
- External MCP servers are passed via --mcp-config to the CLI
- SDK MCP servers use sdkMcpServers in the control protocol (different path!)
- The CLI manages the external server lifecycle, not our SDK
- Need a real or mock external server to test against
- Document the MCP server config format (command, args, env fields)

# advanced_tools.rs — Advanced #[claw_tool] Macro Patterns

## What This Covers
- Vec<String>, Vec<i32> parameters in #[claw_tool] — array inputs
- bool parameters in #[claw_tool] — boolean flags
- Complex return types (multi-content ToolResult)
- Doc comment extraction for tool descriptions
- Tool name inference from function names (underscores → dashes)
- Error handling patterns within tools

## Why This Matters
The existing custom_tool.rs only shows String, i32, and Option<T> parameters. But the
macro supports much richer schemas that users need for real tools:
- Vec<T>: "process these files", "search these keywords", "tag with these labels"
- bool: "verbose output", "dry run", "force overwrite"
- Doc comments: Rust-idiomatic way to describe tools without the name= attribute
- Name inference: Convention-over-configuration (my_tool → my-tool)

The macro tests prove these work, but users won't know without examples.

## Implementation Plan
1. Create examples/advanced_tools.rs
2. Demonstrate tools with:
   a. Vec<String> param: A "batch_process" tool that takes a list of file paths
   b. Vec<i32> param: A "statistics" tool that computes stats on a list of numbers
   c. bool param: A "search" tool with a "case_sensitive" flag
   d. Doc comment description: Tool where description comes from /// comments
   e. Name inference: Function named "my_cool_tool" becomes tool "my-cool-tool"
   f. Complex error handling: Tool that validates inputs and returns structured errors
3. Show the generated JSON Schema for each tool in comments
4. Register all tools in a single MCP server

## API Surface Covered
- #[claw_tool] with Vec<T> parameters
- #[claw_tool] with bool parameters
- #[claw_tool] with doc comment extraction
- #[claw_tool] name inference behavior
- ToolResult::new(vec![...]) — multi-content results
- ToolResult::error() — structured error patterns

## Considerations
- Vec<T> generates {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "..."}} in JSON Schema
- bool generates {"type": "boolean"} in JSON Schema
- Doc comments are trimmed and joined; show single-line and multi-line
- Name inference: underscores become dashes, CamelCase is NOT converted
- Keep each tool small but realistic

title: Example Coverage: Comprehensive API Surface Documentation
description:
# Epic: Complete Example Coverage for Rusty Claw Public API

## Motivation

Rusty Claw is published on crates.io and positioned as the Rust equivalent of Anthropic's
official Python/TypeScript Claude Agent SDKs. The quality and completeness of examples
directly impacts adoption — Rust developers evaluate libraries by reading examples before
reading docs. Currently, only ~40% of the public API surface has runnable example coverage.

## Current State (5 examples)

1. `simple_query.rs` — query() one-shot, message streaming, content blocks
2. `interactive_client.rs` — ClaudeClient multi-turn, connect/send/close
3. `custom_tool.rs` — #[claw_tool] macro, SdkMcpServerImpl, MCP registry
4. `hooks_guardrails.rs` — HookHandler trait, HookEvent, HookMatcher (crate-level only)
5. `subagent_usage.rs` — AgentDefinition, subagent lifecycle hooks (crate-level only)

## Gap Analysis

A systematic audit of every public type, trait, method, and configuration option in the
library revealed 23 distinct API features with ZERO example coverage. These fall into
several categories:

### Session Management (resume, fork, naming, checkpointing, rewind)
These are critical for building production agents that maintain state across restarts,
implement undo/redo, and manage conversation lifecycles. The Python SDK documents these
prominently.

### Safety & Permissions (tool allow/deny lists, CanUseToolHandler, permission handler)
Essential for any production deployment. Users MUST be able to restrict what Claude can
do, and programmatic permission decisions are a key differentiator over the CLI.

### Configuration (system prompts, env vars, CWD, settings sources, sandbox, betas)
Many of these are "first 30 minutes" configuration that new users need immediately.

### Advanced Streaming (partial messages, interrupt, rate limit events)
Required for building real-time UIs, dashboards, and responsive agent interfaces.

### MCP Extensions (external servers, image tool results, mcp_status)
Needed for users integrating with the broader MCP ecosystem beyond in-process tools.

### Transport Layer (direct transport, CLI discovery)
Important for advanced users building custom integrations or testing infrastructure.

## Design Principles for Examples

1. **Self-contained**: Each example runs independently with `cargo run --example <name>`
2. **Commented**: Every non-obvious line has a comment explaining WHY, not WHAT
3. **Realistic**: Use real-world scenarios, not toy "hello world" patterns
4. **Progressive**: Examples build on each other conceptually (simple → advanced)
5. **Top-level first**: All new examples go in `examples/` (the workspace examples crate),
   not in `crates/rusty_claw/examples/`. The top-level is what users see first.
6. **Tested**: Examples should compile in CI (cargo build --examples)

## Success Criteria

- Every public API feature has at least one runnable example demonstrating it
- README.md examples table is updated to reference all new examples
- examples/README.md has a guided learning path
- `cargo build -p examples --examples` compiles all examples cleanly
- Each example has a header comment block explaining what it demonstrates
notes:
## Dependency Graph

```
Tier 1 (Parallel — Start Immediately)
├── rusty_claw-8ju  session_resume.rs         P1
├── rusty_claw-b0q  tool_permissions.rs       P1
├── rusty_claw-e96  partial_messages.rs       P1
├── rusty_claw-xxc  system_prompts.rs         P2
├── rusty_claw-smh  promote crate examples    P2
├── rusty_claw-gvl  image_tool_results.rs     P2
├── rusty_claw-spy  external_mcp.rs           P2
├── rusty_claw-ssj  advanced_tools.rs         P2
├── rusty_claw-dmb  agent_environment.rs      P2
├── rusty_claw-ww3  rate_limit_handling.rs    P2
├── rusty_claw-r51  transport_layer.rs        P3
└── rusty_claw-6v3  advanced_config.rs        P3

Tier 2 (After Dependencies)
├── rusty_claw-zhc  file_checkpointing.rs     P2  ← blocked by 8ju
├── rusty_claw-c9f  interrupt_and_status.rs   P2  ← blocked by 8ju
└── rusty_claw-6ta  hook_callbacks.rs         P2  ← blocked by smh

Tier 3 (Final — After ALL Examples)
├── rusty_claw-b55  Update README.md          P2  ← blocked by ALL above
└── rusty_claw-c8k  CI verification           P2  ← blocked by b55
```

## Execution Strategy

1. **Maximum parallelism in Tier 1**: 12 tasks can start immediately. Group by
   complexity: do the P1s first (session_resume, tool_permissions, partial_messages)
   since they cover the most critical gaps.

2. **Unlock Tier 2 early**: session_resume (8ju) is the key blocker — completing it
   unblocks file_checkpointing and interrupt_and_status. Promote examples (smh) is
   trivial and unblocks hook_callbacks.

3. **README and CI are the capstone**: Don't touch these until all examples exist.
   The README update is the single task that depends on everything.

## Total: 14 tasks + 1 epic = 15 issues
## Estimated new example files: 12
## Current examples: 5 (3 top-level + 2 crate-only)
## Target examples: 15+ (all top-level)
close_reason: All 19 examples are complete, compile cleanly, and are documented in both READMEs. Epic fully delivered.

title: Example: rate_limit_handling.rs — Rate Limits & Error Recovery
description:
# rate_limit_handling.rs — Rate Limit Events and Error Recovery

## What This Covers
- Message::RateLimitEvent(value) — handling rate limit notifications
- Message::Result(ResultMessage::Error { error, .. }) — error result handling
- ClawError variants and recovery patterns

## Why This Matters
Production agents WILL hit rate limits. The SDK receives RateLimitEvent messages from
the CLI, but no example shows how to:
- Detect rate limiting is happening
- Extract retry-after information from the event
- Implement backoff/retry logic
- Distinguish rate limits from other errors
- Recover gracefully (reconnect, reduce concurrency, notify user)

This is critical for production reliability. Without it, agents will crash or hang
when rate limited.

## Implementation Plan
1. Create examples/rate_limit_handling.rs
2. Demonstrate:
   a. A message handler that specifically matches RateLimitEvent
   b. Extracting useful fields from the rate limit JSON (retry_after, etc.)
   c. A simple exponential backoff pattern using the event data
   d. Logging rate limit events for monitoring
   e. Comprehensive error matching showing all ClawError variants
3. Show the full error recovery flow: detect → log → wait → retry

## API Surface Covered
- Message::RateLimitEvent(serde_json::Value)
- Message::McpMessage(serde_json::Value) — mentioned for completeness
- ResultMessage::Error { error, extra }
- ClawError enum variants (all of them with match arms)
- StreamEvent struct

## Considerations
- RateLimitEvent JSON format is CLI-defined; document observed fields
- The CLI handles its own retry logic; SDK events are informational
- Show both the event-level handling and the result-level error handling
- Include all ClawError variants in a match for documentation value
close_reason: Example file exists, is complete (80-180+ lines of working code), demonstrates the described feature with helpful comments

title: Implement McpSSEServerConfig (url, headers) and McpHttpServerConfig for remote MCP servers
description: After implementing stdio MCP servers (McpStdioServerConfig), add support for remote MCP servers via SSE and HTTP. Python SDK defines: McpSSEServerConfig {type: 'sse', url: str, headers: dict|None, timeout: float|None} and McpHttpServerConfig {type: 'http', url: str, headers: dict|None, timeout: float|None}. These enable connecting to MCP servers running in containers, on remote hosts, or as managed services. The CLI JSON format for these is: {mcpServers: {name: {type: 'sse', url: 'http://...', headers: {...}}}}. Implementation follows the same pattern as McpStdioServerConfig but with different field sets. Headers should use a HashMap<String, String>. Timeout should be Option<f64> (seconds). Wire these into the same --mcp-config generation logic as stdio servers. Note: SSE and HTTP MCP servers are increasingly common as teams centralize MCP tooling on shared infrastructure. Having only stdio support would block teams using managed MCP services.
close_reason: Closed

title: Wire mcp_servers HashMap to --mcp-config CLI arg in to_cli_args() and connect()
description: Even after implementing McpStdioServerConfig etc., they won't do anything unless the options are serialized to a --mcp-config argument. This issue handles the CLI wiring. Approach: (1) Add a method ClaudeAgentOptions::to_mcp_config_json() that serializes the mcp_servers HashMap to the JSON format the CLI expects: {mcpServers: {name: {type, command, args, env, ...}}}. (2) In to_cli_args() and ClaudeClient::connect(), if mcp_servers is non-empty, write the JSON to a temp file (or pass inline) and add --mcp-config <path> to CLI args. (3) Temp file approach: use std::env::temp_dir() / uuid-named file, write on connect, clean up on close(). Alternatively, check if the CLI supports inline JSON via --mcp-config '{}' syntax. Important: the existing note in client.rs says 'SDK-hosted MCP servers are NOT passed via --mcp-config; The CLI hangs when type: sdk servers appear in --mcp-config args. Instead, SDK servers are registered via the sdkMcpServers field in the initialize control request.' This distinction must be maintained: external MCP servers (stdio/SSE/HTTP) use --mcp-config, while SDK-hosted servers (SdkMcpServerImpl) use the sdkMcpServers control protocol field. This issue covers ONLY the external server wiring. Depends on McpStdioServerConfig being implemented.
close_reason: Closed

title: Example: file_checkpointing.rs — Checkpoint & Rewind Files
description:
# file_checkpointing.rs — File Checkpointing and Rewind

## What This Covers
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().enable_file_checkpointing(true)
- client.rewind_files(message_id) — revert files to a prior checkpoint

## Why This Matters
File checkpointing is a unique capability of the Claude Code CLI that allows agents to
undo file modifications by rewinding to a specific point in the conversation. This is
critical for:
- Safe code generation (try something, revert if tests fail)
- Exploratory coding (branch, experiment, rewind)
- Error recovery (agent made a mistake, roll back)

No other SDK example demonstrates this. It's a key differentiator.

## Implementation Plan
1. Create examples/file_checkpointing.rs
2. Demonstrate:
   a. Enable checkpointing in options
   b. Send a message that creates/modifies a file
   c. Capture the message_id from the assistant message
   d. Send another message that modifies the same file further
   e. Rewind to the first checkpoint using client.rewind_files(message_id)
   f. Verify the file is back to its earlier state
3. Show how to extract message_id from assistant messages
4. Show error handling for invalid message_id

## API Surface Covered
- ClaudeAgentOptions::builder().enable_file_checkpointing()
- ClaudeClient::rewind_files(message_id)
- Extracting message metadata from AssistantMessage

## Considerations
- Requires ClaudeClient (not query()), since rewind is a control operation
- message_id comes from the CLI's message envelope, not from ContentBlock
- Checkpointing has storage overhead; note this in comments
- Works best when the agent is modifying real files (needs a temp directory)

## Dependencies
- Depends on interactive_client pattern (ClaudeClient lifecycle)
